speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: ...fires in 1910? You mean, what do you mean about it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I mean, I'd heard it was the weather had a lot to do with setting off those fires.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, 1910 was a dry year, you know. The last rain we had was in April. We didn't have no rain from April until in September. But that was a dry year and it was last year, not dry year. Then we had here last year, you know. But nobody ever paid much attention to the river then and the water shortage, you know. We didn't use it, I guess, so much. There was no spraying or no. It's not so much like it is now. Nobody ever thought about the river going dry. I never heard of it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were people pretty worried about the fact that it hadn't rained?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I don't think so. I don't think anybody worried about it. Until that big fire come along, there was fire everywhere, you know. No, I don't think, I don't remember anybody ever talked much about the rain they liked. It could work every day.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember how it got started, the fire of 1910?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think it was lightning, most of it. I think most of them fires were lightning fires. Because,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: them days I don't think people, the room they were on, they brushed so much, they were only cruisers. And they were pretty careful with fires.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I'm pretty sure that, which I don't know, I don't remember just what happened, we never got any details of it. But the fires that I was on, they were lightning fires.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That one that I was on there in, that started on towards, on Tamrock Hill, that's between Park and Southwick.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You ever drove to that, on the road from Helmer to Park?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: This is as far as I've gone to Park.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And then across the Boulder Creek, I can go over to Southwick, go that straight through. Oh, I'd have been there hundreds of times.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What happened at that time? What happened that time?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, they used to,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: that fire started on Sunday, or Saturday, it might have been on Saturday, but if I started, but we didn't get the news to get out and go to it, it'll be Sunday afternoon. They took the old, all them carpenters, you know, they were building the mill there at Elk River. And they took us out, the Milwaukee train tied up there, you know, and they took us out to Camin. If you know where that is, where the old road used to go to Park, the old wagon road, and we walked half away and then we stopped overnight. We took us out in the evening and we went about halfway to Park, walked, and then we got separated. Then we were 22 hours, so we got sent down to the, on the Elk Creek there, to the corral, a little spot fire, you know, a little lightning fire, hit, hit down near the creek bottom. Way down in the hole. And we got that corral then in the afternoon on Monday, is when the fire, the other big fire over, that was bigger on their own barrier, Tamarack Ridge, Tamarack Hill. But that, the wind come up, you know, and then she flared up, and they lost control of it. And it went 20 miles there in two hours. It went from Tamarack Ridge clean over to, oh, let me see now, way past the Jericho Mine, do you hear that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's on the, between Elk River and the North Fork of Clearwater, on the ridge up in there, on Swamp Creek and in through there. But it went 20 miles there in two hours. That's where, that's where we run across Bill Bowles' cabin. We went, when we went to that little spot fire, you know, we passed the cabin and went down in the hole, and we, but we had no heat with this and anything, and we could have that little fire in the afternoon. And we went up there, looked for something to eat, so we went up to Bowles' cabin. Mrs. Bowles, he was there. We had a, we were lucky, we had a cook, you know, the crew. His name was John Kane.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And, and we were down in the hole there and got, after dinner, you know, I don't know what we had, I don't remember what he had, but I think, I think there knocked a few chickens in the head, and they had a big potato patch, whatever plant, and she had bread, so we had a good dinner. And in the afternoon there, when the fire passed us, you know, we come on up, we could hear it, you know, and see it coming, so we run up and got up there again and got, got Bill Bowles. Everything that we could find loose, we dug a hole in and out in the potato patch in the clearing and buried it. Got a cover over that pit again and, they covered it with dirt,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: which never, they didn't come, oh, probably, I would say, oh, close to the clearing, it must have been, when in 100 feet to the clearing, that the fire was, we couldn't, the roar of it, when it went by, you know, you couldn't talk, we had to shout, get right up and shout in the man's ear, you know, to make them ears, it was quite a roar. You see, half of the trees, you know, go, you, you twist it, you know, fire and make it on draft, when they get a big fire. That makes it on draft, you know, when that hip towards the top, you know, boy, that, it's lots of power in it. It, it just twists down to you and break them off and take the top and, and go and I'd carry it for hundreds of feet.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And so, that's through the fire, you know, they throw some sparks and three tops and limbs and off all, they were all burning. So, we look afterwards at just one big flame, all of it. It's quite a sight if, if we'd had a movie out of that, oh boy, if we'd had something.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You've gone down back to work at the spot fire after lunch, and then you heard the fire come. We were going down, we went down to, to kill it out, you see, because, you know, at first we made a chance, made a ditch all around it so they couldn't spread. Down in the hole there was pretty quite, you know, there was no wind there down in the canyon. The wind would hit on the hill, you know. But, so we went back again to Coeur d'Alene, and then we, our idea was to go back, you know, that night and find the main crew again and go up to the big fire. That'd take us all, all the afternoon, you know, to go back. And we thought we'd go down and kill it and be sure that it was out. But we never got to do that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And then we, we stayed there, they come out from, from Louisville, the fire marshal, then the fire warden, that was Frank Mallory. He was fire warden that year.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And this had not happened over there. The husband, Bill Bovill, he was working at Bovill, and the family was out on this home state. Mrs. Bovill and Wallace and Nampa, they were just kids.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So they come out and got him in the evening, and then we stayed, we camped there, you know. Because we had fire then, we had a fire line right there then to work on. So we stayed there as long as we had, and we, I think we had everything that Bill Bovill had in the, in the, in the cabin. And in the potato patch too, dug it out. He had, I think he had plenty of stuff there for the, I don't know, but there was a lot of stuff. We stayed there three, four days, no 22 hours. And that took quite a bit of groceries, but we cleaned them out.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The funny part of it was, because we were all young in that country, and knew, didn't know what well, and we were coming there, and I come in there in the fall of nine. And this was the next summer in August, I think, when the fire was.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And we had, I hadn't been out in the woods there anyhow, but they took it way down to the Belkais place to go where they had, they had established a camp over on the back of Yreka mine somewhere, where it was water where they could take care of a big crew, you know. And we walked down, way down there in the well river in the canyon. Then we had to go back up the hill again. If we, if we didn't know in the country, if they told us, we could have walked over where the main crew was in an hour, and instead of that, we walked, they happened here all day to get to them. We must have walked 30 miles there to get to them, we only was a few miles from them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But none of us knew, knew the country, so we didn't know, we didn't know where to go, we had to follow what they told us, where to go, and had to leave, they had to get us.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We were going home from that fire, you know, over there 10 days, I think somewhere in 10 days. Could have, if we had the end of it done near Song Creek, Cairnbury.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So that fire never took out the bowl cabin?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh no, that long way, some bowl cabin. That would be up there, but it didn't go up there. It would be 27 miles at least. No, we were down towards, this would be somewhere halfway between bowl cabin and a sock, I think, somewhere in that neighborhood. I think it would be closer to a sock, I don't know, it would be up to bowl cabin.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were there other fires that came nearby, like that? You knew where you were working or in?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, what I don't remember, no I don't think so. Because the other big one was way over there on Wallace, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Up on the cemetery ridge.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember them leaving the cabin, the kids and mother?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, it was in the evening,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: on Monday. I couldn't tell you the dates. I don't remember that much.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Don't care about dates. How do you come out there with saddle horses? What about saddle horses?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know if all they went that night, they went to the ..., they had a fire station there and a few bunkhouses. So, no, I don't know if they had any bunkhouses, I doubt it. I think they, oh maybe I'm talking out of turn, I don't remember that too well. But they probably went through Tobogul that evening. That'd be a long ways to close to forty miles.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend a lot of time in 1910 that's fighting fires or was it just that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was further out there, I imagine about ten days I think.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I forget the name of it, I think he was the lieutenant governor afterwards. He was the fire boss. He was Omar Fino. But I forget his name. They had required a camp there established.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: If they packed the, now if they packed the groceries from Melba or from Masaka, I don't know, I don't remember. But the chances are they packed them from Masaka.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: By dent. Could have, maybe both ways.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you go back to working on the mill after?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yeah, we were starting.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Next morning, someone forgot in, you know, we went to work here. They forgot all about the fire.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When did the operation in Elk River really start up?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I mean, when did a lot of people actually start logging out of...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: In 1911. 1911. First they start was there in ten, you know, when they started the mill, that's the time the population started to increase. As they get houses and... As they get their houses built, you know, the workers, married folks, you know, they bring in their family, rent houses. As soon as they get a house built, you know, they're occupied right away.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then they get the boarding house built, coke house started.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they started to build the town. At the same time, they built the mill, they built the town. Leveled up the streets and put in sewers and water mains and whatever have you. They had a little light plant for first. Little small one. I don't know how they run that way. I can't remember now how they run that first little light plant. They had, I guess they must have been steam. They had to run it around the generator.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think they had a little steam boiler. I don't remember that, but that didn't pay no attention to them either too much.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do they spend that whole first summer building the mill up?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, first summer and the first winter. The mill started sometime in the summer of lemon. No, that's quite an operation. Three band sewers, built a dry field, built a planar mill.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was pretty big layout, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: See, after they got started, you know, they handled about 45, 50 million a year, a lumber.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So how long did you stay at Elk River? And work is roused about and all that on there?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I was there from the time, from nine to, the mill went down in 30, 1930. It was the last of it. When they fell down there, just before the case was, 1930s, they never started again.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I was there 20 years, worked on Elk River. Only at the time I put in the Army,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: 17, 18 and 19, I was out of there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I lived there up there till, first wife she died in 1938.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I didn't live there since 1941, was the last I lived up in Elk River.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When did you get married?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Nineteen. About six months after I came out of the Army.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, her wife, she was born in Nebraska.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: She was a sweet little girl.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: She came up there in Franky, in the camp. Nineteen, she came up, she worked, she come from Colville, Colville Idaho. Her mother was cooking in the camp, second cook, and her husband. That's how she happened to come up. She came up there in June and September we married. I took her right away.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He spotted her right off the bat.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I grew up there in Colorado two or three weeks ago. Took those diameters with me. They lived there too, no?

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about Elk River from those early years after the mill was built and the town started up to the war? What was the town like before World War I?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, now I don't know how to explain, or if I could. It was a nice little town, nice little friendly little town. It was just like a big family, all of them. We didn't have a door. Our door was never locked from the time we moved into it in 19th or 1920, I guess.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Up till I left, then in 40th. Didn't even have a key. Never locked the door.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So that was a good little town. Oh, some of the folks come in there in the night, you know, they get

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Ragnar and Hamley and Joe Hamley and Ragnar, Burt Ragnar and let's see who else.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And Socky, Hamley, them three, I don't know, once in a while, they drop in. They come about four o 'clock in the morning, have a little bottle, and come on in. Sometimes they had a little bundle of kindling. We were all using wood then, you know, for the cook stove and all. No, no electrics told them days. And they come on in, they say we brought you a bundle of kindling, so they have to start private in the morning. And they probably had a little bottle of moonshine. It was not, you know, take off. We got to go up and see somebody else. They're going to hook somebody else up. Give them a drink, talk a little bit. Once I had them on Sunday morning. Now they have a pone fee, which was lots of fun.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, they trained crews. They lived mostly, them walking crews, you know, they lived in St. Mary's. But they stay overnight. They're afraid to come up one day and go back the next, you see. One each day. They just turned over, one would come up and one would go down to Mary's. And they want to come up and stay in Elk They want to come up and stay in Elk But they said the Elk They don't put in there all the shopping up in Elk We'll meet since the whole, you know, we're also excited to get social. They said they got better meat in Elk They had Carl Jockak there, you know, he's a good butcher.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What was his name?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Jockak. Carl Jockak, they both did. They died in Lewiston there a few years back. But he had that purchase out there a long, long, long time.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think his daughter lives in, by God, a liqueur up to Clark. That's his first name.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, if you see Mary Fisher, Mary Fisher, by God, you ask about Clark. He worked in the print shop there. They had little newspapers. They called it the Elk River Sentinel.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Dershwin was the big man here, an old fella. And Clark was working with them. Pat, God, I can't.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Is it his wife? No, is it Clark's wife who is Jockak's daughter?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Mary, I think Mary. If I remember. But they live in Lewiston. But this Mrs. Fisher, she know them, and she know a heck of a lot of them. People from up around Bogle, there, she knows everybody.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: She used to write for the Moscow paper, and I think for the review, and I think she did write some for the Lewiston paper, too.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: She's a girl, and now she gets around yet. She gets around pretty good yet.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was Elk River a lot like Bovill, that two towns very much lack her?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No. Well, lean away, at what service...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Both lines. Talking and numbers, that's a guess. And there was bigger at one time, I think there were 1 ,400. When the mail went down. It was quite a little town. Next door, she know the biggest, second biggest town in Clearwater County.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: At one time. But after the mail went down, you see that, that, come all down. Now I'm at in this town, 400 and it's something.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Is that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But it's still little, quite little village.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It's a little village. When I first got there, there was a schoolhouse at Niva. There was another schoolhouse at, oh, out there past, Berlin Creek, Meadow Ridge. And there was another schoolhouse down near Elk River Falls. There were none at, there were none at Elk River when I first got there. There were 10, 3 out there in only, they were homesteaders. And they bested schoolhouses, and got peaches in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Meadow Ridge was quite a little place there. Oh, there must have been 26. 26 to 30 homesteaders out there. When I first come to Elk River. There was a post office in Elk River, call it Trumbull. And, had before, Clearwater County was even started. They started that in 11. Took it out of Nez Perce and, Ada, is it Ada County next? Latah? Yeah, Latah and Ada, is it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, it's down Boise. Yeah, that's an option.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, Shoshone goes down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, Shoshone, there's a clock, that's Wallace.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: They reached over there and got Grangeville, that's in Clearwater, ain't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Or no, no, Grangeville's in Idaho.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, not much of Idaho County then. I think they took it out of two or three counties and made Clearwater County.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And pan and tin can, little coffee and sugar and salt.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And we went down in the middle, and we built a fire. And before we had the fire, after we got the fire, we had to know we had plenty fish to fry. So we had a good dinner down there on the middle. Fish, fried fish.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: This was right after you got there the first time?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: First fall, first fall. That was a nice trout there. And that, you see very lumber yard with Latah, you know, they creek hole just like that, you know. There was meander up through the croquet. And every little puddle was full of fish, full of trout.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What did the country look like then before it was ever logged?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: All timber, all timber, big timber. The town side there was pine trees set three feet down the stump. And you could hardly look at the top of the, there was, there were lots of timber on that, that whole town side was probably millions of feet. That's all out sounded.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Cut it all out, burnt the birds, cleaned it, there was no twig left, we could burn it up.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What kind of pine was it? Was it white pine?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Right pine. White pine, right fern, red fern. Very few yellow pines. There was a few yellow pines up on altitude, but not very many. Time rack, few time racks.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did you get to be boss?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know. That I can't tell you, I was running that myself. No, I had to pick down me. No, I don't know. I often thought, you know, maybe there was something under the hat beside the hair, I don't know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I got along all night.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I seem to have gotten along fairly good with other people, I guess, maybe that was it, I don't know. Because it couldn't be all day, you know, that, when anyway I had, I worked in the timber when I was a kid. With my dad, I was about that high, I guess, you know. Just end up hanging on to the end of the crosscut ground where help with all the trees. And he'd elabored them and cut them off in logs and I, we had to peel them there, see, so I stripped the bark off of them. Then we go and cut another one.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When I was a kid, probably ten year old, maybe nine or ten, first time.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That happened to me.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When I was a kid there, I worked in, I worked in most everything I had there, that patroon, what we had, the big boss. He had a saw milled, a planing milled, had a sash and door factory, had a flour milled, there was a little foundry there, and a machine shop. And then he had a, I couldn't tell you how many acre of land he had, farms he had, he had it divided up in seven passions or sections. No, I couldn't tell you just how much acreage that would be in the fields, but he had seven fields.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: One field was lay in idle every seventh year, that field was idle. Now that was what we call here summer fallow, pastorate, and then summer fallow, then it started in with rye first, planted rye the first year. And when that rye was, when they seeded that, you know, they put in clover and Timothy mixed in it. So the first year had rye, next three years that was hay, that was four of them. And then there was two years, I think he put in oats on it, and the seventh year it was idle again. That's how he worked. So he had a continuous, that was just round and round, and only had one, one of them passion was laying vacant every seventh year. He had worked out a pretty good system there, and he had a good crop. Oh, he had a lot of horses, he had twelve, fourteen head of horses, and he had that many more milk cows.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was the farming end of it. So I worked there on the farm, and I worked in the saw mill, I worked in the floor mill part time. I never worked in the foundry, nor machine trap. And then in the winter time I was out with my dad there to help a dog get timbered.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did he have more horses for his logging operation, aside from the farm horses?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, he used the same one. Used the same ones. Yeah. We used single horses. He'd throw numbers and a single horse. Skittin' saw logs with them. They were pretty handy. One horse, you know, you could do a heck of a lot of work with one little horse. Some of them were big, he had them, I guess they must have had a total ton of them. They were big working horses. Yes, he had one little team that he had for himself, driving horses.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How many families lived on his place, just about?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I couldn't, I don't remember them, but there were quite a few of them. And each and every one of us had a house, you know, and they belonged to him. He had them and he had a place there, and a little piece of land with it, so you had your own garden. And you had the old little field there, so you got up and put in their feed enough so you had a cow and a pig and a sheep or two. So every fall there at home, I remember every fall we had a little yearling to butter and a fair -sized pig and maybe a sheep. That was how we went to meet, for years meet. For that last year, there were no butcher traps, them days.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And he had a smokehouse, and now he had a smoke come out, and you could go over there and smoke it. But most of them days, you know, everybody had to make a brine, you know, salt it down, make a brine, you know, that's the only way, and then hang it up and dry it. That's the only way you could keep meat then, you know, for the summer. We had no refrigeration, none whatever, only ice, you know, take small families, they couldn't have no ice house, you know, to keep that. But the owner there, the patron, what we call him, he had, we took up ice there every year and we had a sawdust pile. Put them, stack that up, you know, clean that out when the ice gets about two feet or three, you know, thick. Then he cut ice and all of them, pile it up, and then put out that, about six feet of sawdust over it. That son would melt it. That's it then, he had ice all summer.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And that's what they done here too, I guess, a long year ago. I don't, I never seen one of those ice houses, how long they did, I don't remember. I don't know how they would have built them here. But, you know, the trains had a lot of ice and towns had a lot of ice, you know, and how that ice house was built, I don't know. And ever since I'd won.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did it work for your family? Did your father get paid so much a year for being there and working?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So much a day, so much a day.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I worked for 20 euro, that'd be five cents a day, ten hours.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What do you like to die for? Five cents a day?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yep, 20 euro. See, that, they, a dollar them days, when I left to own the dollar, I wrote five crowns. And that'd be one fifth of a dollar, see, 20 cents, that'd be one fifth to 20 cents, 20 cents, that'd be five, five cents, I got 20 euro.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did your father make a lot more than that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I guess he got a crown or better or something. We just were small, that's all right, happened to come over here, you know, they sounded better over here.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So when I got big enough and got around to it, I left home, haven't been back, have no desire to go back there either, never had.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I tried it when I was in Germany, but I was in France, but then I had to transfer out of the, after the war, you see, in 19, after the war was over, I was still over there. I tried to attend, but then I had to go and transfer out of the outfit I was in and into the army of occupation, go to Germany first, you see. Then I don't know, then, then I might have got a, could get a chance to get a leave and go across, back over across the Baltic and into Sweden, but I figured I'd been there long enough, I was tired of it, I had enough.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get the schooling there when you were a kid? Did you ever get the schooling in the guy's place?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, you bet you, oh yeah, I got, got through the grade school, that's all the schooling I ever had. And that was, I was two, in 99, I guess, no, 99, 1899 was the last schooling I had.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was 13.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's the end of it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you go very much of the year?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I'm wondering what the schooling was like there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, they had, in the summertime, I guess the school started in the fall, just like here, and then it was out and had a few months or so off, and then we went back to school again for six months or something. Just about half the time, two terms each year. Same as we have here, the same, it's practically the same system. But you know, we had only one teacher for four classes.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He had quite a few kids. There was a man teacher when I went to school.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think I went to school for the same teacher, all the schooling I ever had.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He was an old man when I got through, he had whiskers.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He was a nice old man. We all liked him, and I guess we learned to read and write anyway.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Then after I came here, I learned most of it from the kids. I used to play around with the kids, you know, they were pretty good. Kids are always good. They never get tired, they repeat.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But I couldn't get it right, you know, try to get it right, you know, rest again, and he'd say it again.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Bill Deary?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, not personally, no. I'd seen him a lot of times, you know, because he was a man, he was a big man. He didn't talk to us, he'd puncture them out in the woods. Yes, I'd seen him a few times. That's our old man.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Weyerhaeuser too. Frederick, old man, Weyerhaeuser.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I met him in that river. I was laying track into the plain and meld at that time when he was up there. He came and he had white shirt on and he carried a coat on his arm, he was a grave whisker. That was in 1911.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And Charlie Weyerhaeuser, he was a pretty good gentleman, I just... He'd come quite a... He was the youngest one of the Weyerhaeuser, had three boys. And I don't know how many girls he had there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But John and Charlie and John and... One more. Charlie was the field man, he was the promoter, you might say. And John was the... He had the finance end of it. And what's the two of them's names

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They were Frederick's sons. They were old man Frederick's sons. But one of the Weyerhaeuser's kids... He slept, I had the bottom monk and he had the top monk. Oh, for three months in the camp. One of the Weyerhaeuser's boys. I think that was John's boy. He signed them out there and he stayed in the camp there for about three months.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: To learn what it was like? To learn what it was like?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, it was. I think that was it. And Charlie, the last time I saw Charlie, he was going to send his son, his name was Carl, and stay with me a few months. They were at Daly Creek River. But then that summer, Charlie Weyerhaeuser, he went over to India, I guess up in the... In that country in the islands somewhere, looking for timber. And he got the fever and he came back in the box. So I never seen him anymore, never seen this son anymore.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, so Charlie, he was pretty good. He was a nice old friend. They used to come here once a year. Once a year they come and have a little inspection. Look over and see what we were doing. What we were doing in the woods, in the mills, and they were all the head and manta and the stockholders, you know. They were here, so then sometime we'd fix up a boxcar, not a boxcar, but a flat car and put seats on it. Hooked the old tree onto it and went up the hill and took him out and gave him a good feed in the camp. Sometimes they had a special car, sometimes they took the car, took their car and just hooked on to it. Pulled her up and they gave him a big feed in the camp and back again. They had their meetings in old Potlatch or spookings, but they took a train and then they were old carload out of them, 60 or 70.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did they know much about the operation that they were checking out?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, boy, I tell you they didn't. You wet your own warehouse and they didn't get shanked. Old Charlie there, he was there. When he had that big fire at Clarkia, I put barber's drink. I think he stayed around there for, oh, God, I don't know, for months. Boy, he'd make a little trip out and then he'd come back in and look and see what they did. When the built the incline and all the Clarkia, you heard of it, you and the high sports, you'd never seen it. When you built that incline, I met up and tried to get, they had 32 log -in camps in there the next year after the fire, to try to salvage that timber. See, the fire killed it, you know, and they'd get glued or the beetles, pine beetles get in there and they lose some or some of it. And sometimes if it dried, you know, the bike fell off and it checked. It split. It'd be no good. So he was trying to salvage all that millions or billions of feet sold out. You know, that went to hell. It was a big test, actually. That was in 1922 when he had that big fire.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, when Charlie there, he'd dress up, you know, he had German socks on and rubber boots and an old Mackinac and Malone pants. Looked like, you know, a lump of jack, you know, walking around there. They would tell him, one time he'd come in to Bovill and he, he stayed there in the shack, you know, what they had near there. There was an old shack there near the warehouse where he slept. He went over to Belovac there for breakfast or supper, whichever it was, you know, and they'd always exit every in there. And they'd talk about warehouse, you know, and the tree was sitting there and he was sitting there. Nobody knew him. So he started talking with him. He said, are you working now?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, yes. And I could go and sit there and get the warehouse out, which he said, it never was any good. They don't pay anything. I would say that's what I heard too. He sat there and talked, you know, and he had a heck of a good time of it. He really enjoyed it, you see. And someone after he left and someone said, you know who you were talking to?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No. And he said, I don't give a God damn. He said, that was Weyerhaeuser, a Charlie Weyerhaeuser. Well, he said, that's fine. He said, now that's done, I think I need notes. What I think of him anyway.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Charlie, when he come over, you know, he'd tell it. He'd talk about it. He'd talk about it to the others, you know, that's the thing. And then, keep you in, see, he was the superintendent. I don't know if he was ready to unite with, I doubt it. I don't think he went over breakfast or suffered. I think Charlie was alone. But he'd tell him about it, you know, what that fella said. And laugh, you know what I mean.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You bet he was a good old scout.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, they were good out with the work, warehouse and everything. Fine. True enough, I think they paid a little less and more certain, but then usually when you, you always had a job, if you want to stay with them, there's always a job.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they're still at it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, I told stories about their houses and how they got started.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did they say they got started?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that's hard to tell you whether it's true or not. It's only all that I can tell you stories for Diode.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I'm curious to know, and I don't even care whether or not it's true, I'd like to know what people thought. Because I don't know about it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, if I heard that he'd come over in there, I think, I believe he took a homestead. That was one of the stories. Now, whether it's true or not, that's another, that's the other side of it. But they were telling that he either took a homestead or he bought someone out and bought several of them out. And he got quite a little, little holding and he claimed, we were able to get back to Germany and got some money, come back and bought up some more and built a saw mill.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was the beginning of it, a little saw mill. And then he kept on logging, start logging. They said they didn't pay no attention to lines, I guess, too much, what they tell us. Until they stopped him and they said, okay, sorry, he went over. But that was all there was to it. And I guess he paid little damage to the other homestead. But they were, country wasn't, had to be in the early days that they were out there whenever that was. Because you were there long times before me.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: My God, I don't know if I ever heard of air out there when I was in Minnesota. I don't think so.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Until I come out here to Washington.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think part of that was the first one I ever heard of.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And warehouse were only owed, what they tell me, that when they start beginning the owed, he had 49 percent. And the Laird Norton family had the other 51 percent stock. But you know, they come up there every year, they were out there. Really was the one that started it. Dear, he was the first one out there to get located. Crucier, I'm more or less than he got to be. With the first manager, he built it. Laird was assistant manager. And he was a stockholder too. So they had family. Laird and Norton family, I think it was.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There was another nice man with the Laird. E. W. Laird.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I've heard that he put a lot of his own time and effort into building up Potlatch.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He really took an interest in the town as well. No, they did.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I heard, I don't know whether they were through or not. I never ran into anything. I ran from the beginning to the end. Now you know it's an open town. It's so old. I think each family that's working there, I think each one bought their own house. I believe that's open town now. Before that, you know, it was a company town. Didn't sell anything. Nobody could hold any property in town.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they had, like, dirty showers, you know, that was a big one. The manager and the assistant manager and on down, a little lesser, you know, down to the little fella. I think the smaller one, they paid about $20 or something a month, something like that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And the other should pay a little more because the bigger the job you know, then you had to move over. If you got promoted, you moved over to another house. Paid a little more.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: And the house was a little bigger?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, yes. It's over there. You only had an uphill there too, you know, where the higher ones lived. None of the little fellas near them.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So you got to know Mr. Laird pretty well, huh?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, fairly well. I talked to him a lot several times. He had to move down to Spieder with them. She was running around over the rails. And lucky enough, I never got off the track. It's a bit of a hurt to many.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But we had quite a few trips on the little Spieder, on the rails.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, he was a pretty nice old man.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What do you think Potlatch thought of these IWWs and the labor troubles when they started the management?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know. They didn't work.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The idea was to try to hold them out as much as you could. The upper, the higher reps, I don't know, they didn't have too much to do. They'd come back onto us, little chops out in the birch. We were the ones I had to put up with it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing at that time? What was your job, yeah?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I was kind of a foreman.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were you by then?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, I was in that category since 1910.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I was there about eight, eight of it, eight of it, eight of it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I got along pretty fair. I never got any Lincoln.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had quite a few dispute to talk about it. I tell them what I thought about him and what I felt to hold him. But it never got to any blows.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember about when you first started hearing about the IWW in the woods?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, let's see. That must have been drawn well after the mill started there in Elk over.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: See, the first strike we had there was in, no, the first really strike we had there was in 17, just when the First World War, you know, in 17. That's when they had the big strike, the first one. When they really broke out, I think every camp and mill here in the inland was right down to that strike. See, they struck there. Every camp from Elk River down to St. Mary struck the same day. Every camp, every one of them. And they were the big companies.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And Diamond Match and Ohio Match.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And you had Herrick.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I forget all of them. They were several down there in St. Mary's. And Cardellines. But they had that all time, you know. So every camp. And then, because when the camp shut down, this almost did too. They were all lost.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: You knew they were going to strike ahead of time, didn't you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, yes. I knew it. I knew it. I worked with them. I was right there. I was in the camp, you know. I knew it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They were striking on a Monday. So, Sunday I went down. Oh, I camped just about four miles out of Elk River. We had a lot of snow that went there. But we worked in the horses in the 12 feet of snow. Log down went there. But in the spring there, you know. But I knew it before. As I was lucky enough, I, on Thursday, before that, I removed the camp in there in the snow in December, I guess. Whenever it was somewhere before Christmas. And we worked all the way there. So I burnt all the lashing, all the brush. All of it. Took the whole crew there one afternoon. It was nice, you know, Sunday and warm in the first part of June or May. And I burnt all my brush and got it put out. So I didn't have any fire. I said that was one thing. I knew they were going to strike. And we logged a couple days after that. Sunday I went downtown, you know, because then the upper, they all knew it. Everybody knew it. You know, that it was common. There was no secret. And I was deputized. Deputy Sheriff. Sheriff was out there in town and I got a gun and an combination for something. I don't know what for. So I come up to camp again and the sheriff made out a little proclamation, a little notice, and I put up that I kneel up at the camp, you know, that everything is disturbing or anything. They wouldn't stand for any part of that. I got back to camp, you know, I have a sticker I WW on every stump, every car, cars, and wherever they could hang one, you know, there was a sticker there. I got up there to the barn first and the teams, they were all sitting there and the stickers all around them. And I asked them, I said, what the hell don't you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, that don't look very nice. I didn't want to take them off. I said, this is our home. And I said, you should be working here. We're living here. All of us, me too. And I said, I don't like that junk sticking around. Come up to the camp, you know, they were sitting there, buns here and the buns there all around the camp, you know, and sending them stuff from town stumps. So I asked them the same thing and I said, you know, this is our home. I said, your home and my home. And I said, we don't need that stuff here. I said, we know what's up. And I said, I know it and you know it. I said, why don't we don't need that stuff?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, tomorrow to take it off. I said, it'll look better. And I had 20 boxes of dynamite laying in plain sight there. We sent up the day before. I was worried. I didn't feel any damn good. But they were driving in and I had a cup of coffee in the kitchen and they come out again and looked down, you know, bank yard. They took all the stickers. They took them, they cleaned them off.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Why? I don't know. But they took them. They disappeared all over them. They were sitting there talking. But they had to pick them all off. It looked pretty clean. Before breakfast the next morning, you know, bank yard, they come and start it in. I don't think I was out of bed when they come in and they wanted their time. I said, I'm going down. I'm going down. I said, dear. I said, of course I know. You're all at the time. And I said, why don't you breakfast when you're ready?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We'll be in a few minutes. I said, when you get out there, tell them not to bother. And they said, wait, wait and give us a chance. And I said, wait till after breakfast. And come on in. I said, we'll try to get you fixed up as fast as you can. All of you. We'd work out. And I said, we're all going in. And we still do the math. Wait a while and I'll give us a better chance in here. Sooner or quicker you get out of here, don't come and bother us. By God, you know, they spread that new shadow. They left us alone. About noon. All we had to do was just make out a time slip and they paid at the main office downtown. You see, they already got their paycheck. And it wouldn't take them long there. You know, they had the time slip in front of them and they were savages. And it was all failed out and all they had to do was make the check like that time slip and then go to the next one. What did you lose?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So it was over that afternoon or all too. But no matter where there was a few, I had two brothers working there and this Oscar Agbombe and the two or three more that was old -timer that had been with me there for years. So I told them I'd send slip out, take your, take your, and going up the creek, we moved. There was a cabin up at the at creek there a little bit and a few miles up the river and I said slip out and get over there and say, take your fish out with you. But get out of here. He was a loner and said, what you gonna do?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, I was there to get fixed up. Then I come up and see you. I come up and have a feed with you and I bring some bread and stuff and bacon and so forth, you know, they can't fry fish. You gotta have a little grease.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I had, when the strike was over, I had eight men, I think, left or they hold crew. And bang gully there for a month,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: through the rest of the June and you lie. Then I had, then when we fought fire, the others didn't, you know, what the idea was, what they used to tell us that they would try to burn us out. Put to my camp, it's okay. I didn't have any brush to set fire to. It's all out. But the other camp wasn't quite so fortunate. So we fought fire. We worked 16, 18 hours every day. We had a train crew and we run from one place to another, from one fire to another. Each day we leave at three o 'clock, you know, and come home after dark, clip the couple outs and go and get out again. Then I went and enlisted in the Army of Connecticut. I went down and enlisted in the Army of Spokane.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What camp was this?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was Camp B. The Louisville side, you know, there were Potlatch and the Elk River side. There were Potlatch too, you see. There were two separate outfits. So, Louisville had numbers and our camp was lattered. Horswyday, B .C. and D .C. and in Bovill there were one, two, three and four. Otherwise, you know, they ordered so it would be mixed up.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where did all those men go once they struck?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, yeah, and it's Spokane, most of them. Spokane, the Spokane, St. Mary's, Cardeline.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: And was it some of the strikers that were setting all the fires that you had to fight?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, not necessarily. No, I don't think so. I don't think they sat any further, as I know of. No, they sprung up, you know, sometimes lightning, etc. No, we were fortunate enough, far away there. I don't think anybody ever set a fire that I knew of. But that afternoon, you know, I picked out what I thought was the leaders in the IWW's. And I gave them a half a dozen boxes of matches. No, we got matches, boxes that big then, you know. They used to charge a nickel for them. Either Ohio match or diamond match, they made them. So I gave them five or six boxes of them, you know, and designated so many men, ten or twelve men with them. I said, you take that and go up that door. Anything you can see that would strike a set fire. It was set fire to everything. By God, that afternoon, they burnt their whole damn trashing. They burnt all of it. So we were pretty clean, and they had two more days, you know, to kill it off. There was any snag left, any of the butts burning or stumps or something. So I was clean. I didn't have any fires.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, by golly, I've got to tell you, I had a night watch. I usually had it, well, in the summer, always in the summertime, we had a night watch around the camp, you know, going in the water for five years or anything that happened. And he was an old sheriff, used to be. I couldn't tell you his name now, but anyway, after that, so I put him on there, he was night watch. And I had an old 30 -30 rifle, and I gave it to him. He was out there all by himself, and you didn't know somebody come up. There were a couple of times, if you come up to the camp, but I'd watch him in the day. In the daytime, too, kind of a guard, you know, for a few days, so nobody come up and do any harm to any of us. So I walked down there one night, you know, and I didn't come back. There must have been towards midnight when I was hiking up the railroad track. And I had forgotten this whole night watch I had. And for some reason, when I come walking, you know, I start to whistle and sing or something.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And all that once and always said, is that you, ...? And I said, yes. And I said, where in the hell are you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And he said, I'm here. He said, God damn you. He said, if... And he was back of a stump. He said, I had that rifle lined right in your gut. He said, if you hadn't started to whistle, he said you would have got it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So that, that, I guess maybe that saved me that time. Whether he had shot or not, I don't know, but he set up what he did. He said, I had that line right on your gut. He says, if you hadn't whistled.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But he said, when you start to whistle and over sing or whatever I did, you know, that, that showed that I had no, there was no intention I wanted to be here.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now there were one more time I had that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But then that was where that was in a fire. I know what the hell was that time. I was up in the upper basin there, up at the askiver, and we broke down. One of them engines broke down the side rods on the little locomotive. They were coming up the hill and all they broke, oh, whatever you call it, on the, they stood, that holds the side rods on the wheel. That broke off and also one fell down and it bent, broke, or it didn't break, but it bent it. So we couldn't use it, see. I took it off and I had it in the pickup and drove across the hill. And I think that was the fire patrol that had the guard up there that had let nobody come in and they had to fire dry season. That fella's name was Jackson and his partner was Hayes. Two of them, they had a tent up there. Now I'd forgotten all about them. I never thought about them. But they had a chain across the road there so nobody could come, go up in the timber, you know, picket berries, or they had it blocked off. And they were kind of guarding it, you know, on that main road. And I saw the light, I had a brakeman with me, named Shelton, and I was driving and I saw the tent, you know, the light was on and a little bit, you know, I see it has got little close, you know, they turned off the light. But I had a headlight on the pickup, you know. So we got up against the gate where they had this chain across and Shelton, he was a young man, you know, because he was an old buck, you know. He said, oh, you want to go out and open the chain?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, no, no, wait, wait. And I said, I'll go. And I said, I want to talk to him anyway. So for some reason, he's still going over to the tent. I walked up and I walked in front of the pickup, you know, in the light. And I stood there a little bit, you know, the light was on. So I was standing in the red in the light, you know, and I couldn't see anybody. And somebody hollered, he said, do you ask him?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, yes. And here they come, one had a 30 -30 and the other one had a double band shotgun. They were on on each side of the road. But if I'd have left there, Shelton would know about it, you see. And so, then he was shaking. He was pretty nervous, that old man, Jackson. I was very in the tent with him and talked to him a while. And he said, you know, he said, there was somebody had a call and they said they had, in the camp up there, they had a poker game on and somebody come in and held up the table. And they called through and they said, if time signs are gone, come over there, you see who they are. And I didn't know anything about it, you know, by golly, that was Helmer. Bill Helmer had done that, called Jackson. I did most for fun, you know, because I borrowed his pickup to go over and he called ahead there and on the hyper dome, we were both on the same line, same phone in our camp, you know, and he went in there and called and got Jackson on the phone. But I don't know whether that was done for fun. But when I come back, Jackson, he told me, my God, he said, when you come back, I had to go to a shop, you know, and get this thing straightened under a steam hammer, you know, get it hit up and then put the weight on it with the hammer, press it down till you get it, and that was kind of a straight edge on that table, you know, where the hammer would. It didn't take too long. So I was caught in a dry pen when you come back again. And he was, by God, I come back in a couple of hours, you know, that old man was still sitting on the bed the same way that I went and he was still shaking. Whatever his intention was, I don't know what he'd have done if it was in a strange area. I don't know. But he was a little, kind of a little nutty anyway, the old man, I don't know. But he was more scared than I was. I wasn't scared of getting there. Didn't bother me. But he was still sitting there on the head and he even laid down. He's still sitting on the same place a couple of hours later.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Bill Helmer was just joking?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I think so. He'd done it for a yoke, you know. But I tell you now, I put a scare in the helmet after that. I said, you better look out. I said, I wouldn't. I don't know. I don't know what the hell that man was. I can't handle the resistance. I don't know what he could have done. And I said, he sure got it in for you now, Bill, because you scared him so much. Now, thank God I don't think Helmer went back for three weeks or more. But he surely went in daylight the next time he went over.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did they solve that strike? How did they work out the differences between the men and the management, you know, the wobbly strike in 1917?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know. I don't know. I don't know. I wasn't here. I was in France when that was over. No, I don't know that. But they went out. But after that, when I came back again, we had, there were no more packing blankets. That was out. Company finished blankets and clean sheets, linen sheets and pedal slips. And the big camp cars, you know, where we used to sleep, 12 to 16 men, they had a petition of four. There were four to each, four times four. No, wait a minute.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There were two, two in each. They were, they cut it down to eight men, I guess, or half or more. Instead of having a whole, half a camp crew in one car, you know, they were down to so many. And there were the bunks, bunks on both sides. And they had a table in front. And they put up showers in the camp and hot water, so you take three, four showers in each camp, you know. So the three or four could take a shower at the same time. So that was a good blessing that they, and that was the wobbly one out there for conditions.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they had a lot cleaner. And I think they, I believe the company finished, no, I don't think they finished towels.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I guess they had their own soap and towel or something.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Had the conditions been pretty tough before that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Was there a lot of... Oh, yes, it was. No, there was... Yes, there was. At times they were real tough. I know how to... They camped full of them. And later, and same at Al Curver, that was all new, but they didn't take long, you know, for some reason. Bed bugs got in pretty bad. But we got ready to do them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: First we tried to steam the cars, and that was no good. That was no good either, you know. You could sit there and put the steam pipe in there and set the locomotive alongside of it and close the doors and windows. And they steamed them until that pitch was running on the outside of the car, but outside wall. But still, that seemed to force out all the nets and all that's in the cracks, you know, between the lumber. So in a month or so, they were worse than was before. So the later years, when they used the cars, we painted them every year. That took it out. You'd light a turpentine and you painted all the cracks full, you know, or they couldn't come out. That paint, you know, you fill that cracks, you know, full, you paint and dry that set, you know. So we got ready. There were no bed bugs anymore.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I think they had to do the wobbly there in the war days, the first one in the war days. They did a good job there. Before that time, each one of us carried their own blanket from camp to camp, you know. There was no way anybody could keep a camp clean, because, you know, they go from one camp to another and unload the bundle and that might have been full of bed belts in this state. Or maybe we got a new batch and they walked from our camp to another one.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: How do you want... Oh, I was just wondering about the... You mentioned there was dynamite in that it came into the camp there and you were worried about that.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Whatever happened with the dynamite?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, nothing happened to it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, the way we got it, you know, the place to destroy, but there was nothing could fall in any better place. It was a good place for them and to have a ton of dynamite in front of them. If they had the notion to do any damage to us, that was a heck of a big help for them, you know. There was nobody ever had any, nothing come out of it. And there was no place to put them. We had no way to put them. So I left them there. They were gone. I didn't want to go out there in front of them. I carried it, tried to carry it to any father away from the camp, you know. That would be more or less a help, you know, that showed that I was afraid of it. I left the joint there. I was worried about it, but that's all. No, no, they didn't do no harm to us, no any of us.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So you think that they didn't really intend to do any harm?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't think that crew did, no. No, they didn't either. They didn't do no harm. I don't know what they did in other places, I don't know, but they were set in fire and so forth, you know. That was supposed to be the aim of it, but then whether it was or not, I don't know. But that was the impression that they gave to us, for a month there, to be on the lookout for it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But we had no trouble. I didn't, and I don't know, I never heard of any either, because the techniques and news didn't go as fast around as it does now, from one place to another. All we had was the newspaper and the phone. Had no TVs, no radios.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And the phone, most of the time, was out. Fire broken.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were the guys who were the wobbly leaders, what were they like? Were they good workers, most of them? Were they leaders?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, they were. They were. They were.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They were good men, but they, you know, they, I don't know what they, they were trying to build, the damn days they said they won, they won. Some more on them. When I come back, they had, when I come back from France, they had

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: the loyal legion and loggers and lumbermen, the four L's. They got loyal, legion, and loggers and lumbermen. And most of them called it the loyal legion. They said, they loyal legion, they're loggers and ladies. They got bronchos in, they came. Loyal legion, they're loggers and ladies, that's the way it do. Well, they didn't. Then, you know, they worked again and let, so the wobbly's wouldn't come in there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The wobbly's wouldn't stay if you told them that they belonged to them. They had to, they had to, in order to get a job in the camp, they had to sign up, you know, in this new union, company union. That didn't go very good. I don't know how they had it. The army come up there. That was more or less from the army, I guess. I guess they were telling me that the army officer come up and kind of have an inspection now and then through the camps. During the war, which I don't know anything about. But I had to join the two when I come back. It's okay.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did those guys, did they sing songs, the wobbly's? Did they have wobbly songs?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know. I suppose they did, yes, I think so. They did, I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I'm real curious about how you felt about that because it seems like on the one hand you were management, on the other hand you sort of had a lot of respect for that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was standing between the management and the wobbly's. When you were working, when you were forming in the camp, you got to try both ways. You got to try to get along with the crew and you got to try and do what the company asked you to do. You can't, you're standing there between them. You're sitting on top of the fence.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You can't jump off on this side. You can't go, you're trying, you're mediating, you're in between. And you just have to try to do the best you can and the best you know how. Try to do your level -headed best. Because you've got to try to, when you're working, you've got to, you know you've got to try it. You've got to try and hold the crew.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And at the same time you've got to try to do what the company expects you to. Most of the time you have to use your own judgment. You have to. Because you can't go to the letter T, either one way or the other. The company tells you one thing and back out you know that maybe against your own way of thinking and you still, you still got to work with those people. And try to get something out of it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So they're foremen and them they choice to sit between the devil and the deep sea.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did the company get you on the carpet at all for not doing what they wanted you to do?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I was on the carpet a lot. I guess more than anybody else I think. Yes, you bet you I was on the carpet.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Because you know you've got to show favors. You can't have any pets when you're a foreman, that's for wanting. But you know you better, in order to hold the crew you've got to try to be as fair as you can. Both ways, both ways. You've got to be fair to the company and you've got to try to be fair to the labor. And a lot of times you know your judgment is not exactly what the company says, because you're on the carpet. Explain why in the hell was that done? What did you do that for?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was against our... You don't know when you get out there in the morning and face a bump like that? Or more? More or less?.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sometimes a lot of times you feel guilty and a lot of times you think, by God you're doing the right thing.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yesterday was hot, every hot time. We had a little strike at the last... ...how much of a tea every day and... ...they walked out at the last robin strike. That was in 48, I believe.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We walked out and we... Oh, we were 5 '6", I was left there. They were all monthly men. Me and a couple assistants and... I think I had the mechanic left and...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I believe there was a... We had one kind of a watchman, every night, stay down. We worked a little, what we could do was fuse. We all worked a little bit and played a little bit too. That strike was never ended, but we finally started working again and they come back, got them back again. Most of the old fellas come back in about a month or two. But I grew up in 2 -3 months there, in the summertime just a few of us. Pecacarabella, they said, do a little fishing and do a little work.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: When you came back after the war, were there many of the same guys back?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: In that particular camp where I came back and started in, on the same camp where I left off before they moved. No, the old crew that I had, they were all gone. Yes, there were one or two left, I think. The old barn bullets was left.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: And they left the country entirely?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh no, the old, they were in there. I got them back in about three weeks that I had. I had most of my old crew back again. And then for the first summer in 20, I had about 25 or 30 of my old buddies in France come and work for me, work with me again. The old buddies I had in France in that outfit over across the water, had quite a few of them, a couple of dozen or more. But then in 21, it was something hit there and the lumber went haywire, so it was shut down. Mail and all, there was no trip and the freight rate went up for one thing on lumber. The Potlatch had their orders that deep, one day for lumber. Next morning they had nothing. Everybody cancelled it. They wouldn't pay that freight rate. So we were shut down there for three or four months and I lost them all, because then they all scattered again, all over. So I didn't have very many left. They didn't come back then, because they'd go and get jobs. They haven't had any place they could then after they had to.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We were quite a few there from Elle Creek. We were not too many.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think I wanted to take it. Maybe about a dozen of us had enlisted. Most of them, you know, that immediately they was not citizens. You know, in the woods there in the timber, they were mostly foreigners working. Most of us. I was included. I didn't have much citizen paper here. I wish I'd had. I'd have had a long race in the Army if I'd had them. But I didn't.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Even with what little schooling I'd had, I'd have made it all right there. But I was not a citizen, so I couldn't get a commission, you see. Couldn't hold one. Couldn't get one. Till the last few days of October, then I was holding in as a citizen. They come up to camp and throw me in, in France. And then I was, then if the war had lasted another two months, I'd have been a second lieutenant.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But the armistice was declared that. They'd have to live until a month. Two weeks after I got to it, or three weeks after I had my persona in the citizen. And they had a summary court. Colonel two major, two captains, and two first lieutenants come up in the camp where I was. Up near the border of Switzerland. Up in the corner there near the L .O. Rain. Way up in the Ura Mountains. And they come up there from Benanci, or from Pershing's headquarter, which they were waiting for in the near -senway.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But I didn't get it. No more promotions after the 11th. That was out the next day on the bulletin.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But if I'd have had the message and papers, you know, I'd have got promotion in the field.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: I want to talk to Axel about, huh?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I want to talk about the war a little later. Before I talk about the war or more, I'd like to know, I was thinking, maybe you could describe to us some of what the logging operations were like in the teens. When you got out there and out of the mill and started logging in the woods, what methods they were using then, and what you remember about some of the different operations.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, they had the first, when they were, the first in 11, 12, 13, 14. I was building railroads. I was building roads. And for them to set camps and all get equipment out. All the logs come in on rails then, you see. No logs. Logging with horses and steam donkeys. And they had, they had a lot of timber they owned up in their, on state and government land. And that had to be logged before a certain date or it'd go back. The company had bought the timber, but they didn't buy the land, you see. And they're so long to take the timber off. So I was busier than a dick, you know, lazy trekking and set a donkey or a team camp or whatever they had. And get them timbers off from certain sections or certain forties or eights or whatever the acreage was. And then, as soon as they were through there, pick that steel out and put it someplace else. So I was laying rails all the time, you know, building road and laying rails all the time. Pick up whatever they were doing, you know, put up someplace else because there was another part of it that had to be logged right away. That lasted for over, for five years before I got caught up with it. I didn't get into the logging until in 15. Between the time that I was working on the rails and steel and the railroads.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Were you in charge of the crews that were laying the tracks?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, and building the road bed too. Build the road bed and then put the rail on. So I had a train crew. I had a train crew with me on the camp on rails, you know. We probably worked here tonight, you know, and after supper, pick up the little platform we had between the bunk cars and the kitchen cars, you know. As you tighten the car, you know, to carry the center. You know, the type of boys who could tighten an old flat car level otherwise it's safe, you know. But we stick all that underneath the ten bunk cars, the steps could come up to them in the platform. And after supper, and moved probably in the morning, we were ten miles, we were ten miles away from where we had supper, where we had breakfast the next morning. We had a water tank with us, we had water with us, filled that thing, water tank. And for the kitchen and the bunk cars, you know. So no matter where we was, we had water. We had always packed a bunch of wood with us, no matter where we were cutting the car, so we always had wood and water and groceries, you know, with us. So we might work over there in Eva in the evening and the next morning, you know, when we had breakfast we were way up El Crick, you know, about the summit there, up El Crick somewhere, ten, fifteen miles away from the food camp. So I had that for a year from eleven to fifteen when they come. The boys come over there one day and he said, you come in with me, he said, how would you go up to Camp B, that was, take that out there, was sitting in town and, he said, you take that out in the morning, go up there and run that camp, log it next to it. And I don't know nothing about it at all, you know, nothing about it. And I said, what's the matter with that fellow you had last?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, he said, he'll be scaring, he needs to go back scaring. So I said, is that it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said, yes. Well, I'll try to get that to you, that's it, that's all right, I guess I'll go with you. Try it. I have no one thing about it but I'll do my best. So in I went, you know, next day we put out another new camp, you know, set. I looked fine and I kept on logging from that time on. 15, 16, and up into 17, two years that I was in there before the war broke out, before we got into it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Who was on this railroad crew, were they Italians or were they... Oh, everybody.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Swedes, Norwegian, Danish, Germans, Frenchmen, Italians, Turks. They even had an Indian with a big blanket around his head.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Montenegro, Serbians, Austrians. Oh, I can't name all of them, they were, they were all mixed. All the immigrants, all come from the foreign countries. Oh, a few of them, there were a few old timers, that was a few Irishmen there in Scotland. I longed all of them, best I could. Some time I fired the old outfit, the next day it would come up. I had an employment officer in his bookend called in, they called in, and all the others. Federsdon, he worked for a part of that, he was an employment agent, in the area down there on Main,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: but on the street there, between Main and Front or Trent. I called in there, next day he come up with a whole 60, 70 item.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What would you fire your whole crew for?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, take them out, we had a new crew, we didn't get along anymore, so. Take them out. Couple days and they had a new crew again. That happened, they were shortened.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But then after a few years, that was in the beginning, after a few years, and oh my God, they started to know them, and they started to know me pretty well, so they stayed. My God, when I was shot down there in the last two or three years there, for more, before the war, I had the same crew. If you shut down for snow, not too much in the winter, you couldn't build roads in the 68 feet of snow. If you shut down for a couple of months or three, by God in the spring they were all there again. They all come without asking, by God they come. So we hardly ever use the employment officers anymore. They all come back.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Now in the early days, would you fire them or would they quit on you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, they quit on me too, not all the crew, but they, some of them quit, you know, they quit, sure. That's natural. One will quit, one or two. But no matter how you try, you know you can't shoot all. The foreman can't shoot all. That's impossible. You've got to, somebody thinks you're a SOB, sure. And they said, I wouldn't work anymore for that, but that's it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But if you fire 50 or 60 at once, they must have been doing something you didn't like. Well, that, you know, they get this wobbly idea that I won't work.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No work. Slow down, slow down, you see. But then, you know, I had to hold my job too. And if you don't get anything done, I'll be the one to go. When I figure out, by God I'm going to stay.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I had to fire them out. We had to train crew to start wheeling them into town. The trampolines were all made up. Most of the time I went and rode in with them. Took a couple of flight cars. Load them all up and go into town, they said. So they could be paid off. Then I walked back to camp again. You know, that happened a few times.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: How did you lay the steel?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The tracks.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What did you use to lay them with?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: By hand.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Spine. Carry them. Carry them. Carry the rails out, carry the ties out. We had to load it on trot cars, you know. And we had to roll us on the side of the car so we loaded them back in.
